rules.description: Container is running in privileged mode
rules.risk: Grants full access to all host devices and disables most kernel isolation. An attacker inside the container can trivially escape to the host.
rules.remediation: Set securityContext.privileged to false. Use specific capabilities instead of privileged mode.

rules.description: Container may run as root (UID 0)
rules.risk: Processes inside the container execute as UID 0. If the container shares the host user namespace, a breakout grants immediate host root access.
rules.remediation: Set securityContext.runAsNonRoot to true and securityContext.runAsUser to a non-zero UID.

rules.description: allowPrivilegeEscalation is not explicitly disabled
rules.risk: Processes can gain more privileges than their parent via setuid binaries or kernel exploits. The Kubernetes default is true, which is permissive.
rules.remediation: Set securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation to false.

rules.description: Container has dangerous kernel capabilities added
rules.risk: Dangerous capabilities such as SYS_ADMIN, SYS_PTRACE, SYS_MODULE, NET_RAW, or NET_ADMIN greatly expand the attack surface, enabling kernel module loading, process tracing, and network-level attacks.
rules.remediation: Remove dangerous capabilities from securityContext.capabilities.add. Use only the minimum capabilities required.

rules.description: Capabilities are not dropped with DROP ALL
rules.risk: Without explicitly dropping all capabilities, the container retains the default capability set which may include unnecessary and exploitable capabilities.
rules.remediation: Set securityContext.capabilities.drop to ["ALL"] and then add back only specific capabilities required.

rules.description: Read-only root filesystem is not enabled
rules.risk: An attacker who gains code execution inside the container can write to the root filesystem, enabling persistence or binary replacement.
rules.remediation: Set securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem to true. Use emptyDir or tmpfs volume mounts for writable paths.

rules.description: Seccomp profile is set to Unconfined
rules.risk: Without a seccomp filter, the container process can invoke any syscall the kernel allows, expanding the attack surface for kernel exploits.
rules.remediation: Set securityContext.seccompProfile.type to RuntimeDefault or Localhost with a restrictive profile.

rules.description: Pod shares the host PID namespace
rules.risk: Host PID namespace sharing exposes all host processes to the container. An attacker can inspect, signal, or ptrace host processes, leading to credential theft or privilege escalation.
rules.remediation: Set spec.hostPID to false or remove it entirely.

rules.description: Pod shares the host network namespace
rules.risk: Host network namespace gives the pod full access to host network interfaces and services bound to localhost, including kubelet API, cloud metadata service (169.254.169.254), and etcd if co-located.
rules.remediation: Set spec.hostNetwork to false or remove it entirely. Use Kubernetes Services for networking.

rules.description: Pod shares the host IPC namespace
rules.risk: Host IPC namespace allows the container to read and write shared memory segments of host processes, enabling data exfiltration or tampering.
rules.remediation: Set spec.hostIPC to false or remove it entirely.

rules.description: Pod mounts a dangerous host path
rules.risk: Mounting sensitive host paths (/, /proc, /sys, /dev, docker.sock, containerd.sock, /etc/kubernetes, /var/lib/etcd) breaks container isolation and enables container escape, credential theft, or full host compromise.
rules.remediation: Remove hostPath volumes for sensitive paths. Use emptyDir, configMap, secret, or PVC volumes instead.

rules.description: hostPath volume is not mounted read-only
rules.risk: Writable hostPath mounts allow the container to modify host files. An attacker can tamper with host binaries, cron jobs, or configuration files.
rules.remediation: Set volumeMounts[].readOnly to true for all hostPath mounts that do not strictly require write access.

rules.description: Service account token is auto-mounted
rules.risk: The service account token is mounted at a well-known path. If an attacker gains code execution, they can immediately use the token to query the Kubernetes API with the service account's RBAC permissions.
rules.remediation: Set automountServiceAccountToken to false unless the workload needs Kubernetes API access.

rules.description: Container uses hostPort
rules.risk: hostPort binds a container port directly to the node IP, bypassing Kubernetes service abstractions and exposing the port to anyone who can reach the node.
rules.remediation: Remove hostPort. Use NodePort or LoadBalancer services, or an ingress controller.

rules.description: Container has no resource limits defined
rules.risk: Without resource limits, a container can consume all CPU and memory on the node, causing denial of service for other pods or triggering OOM kills.
rules.remediation: Set resources.limits for cpu and memory on all containers.

rules.description: Systemd service unit does not set a non-root User directive
rules.risk: The kubelet or K8s-related process runs as UID 0 on the host. Combined with a container escape, this yields immediate root access to the host.
rules.remediation: Add 'User=<non-root>' to the [Service] section of the systemd unit file that starts the Kubernetes component.

rules.description: Volume mount uses Bidirectional mount propagation
rules.risk: Bidirectional mount propagation allows mounts created inside the container to propagate to the host filesystem. An attacker can mount filesystems on the host from within the container, enabling container escape.
rules.remediation: Set volumeMounts[].mountPropagation to None or HostToContainer. Remove Bidirectional unless strictly required.

rules.description: Container image uses :latest tag or has no explicit tag
rules.risk: Without a pinned image tag or digest, the container may pull a different image version on each restart. An attacker who compromises the registry can push a malicious image with the same tag (tag mutation attack).
rules.remediation: Use a specific image tag and ideally a digest (e.g., image:v1.2.3@sha256:abc...). Avoid :latest or untagged images.

rules.description: AppArmor profile is set to unconfined
rules.risk: Disabling AppArmor removes mandatory access control enforcement, allowing the container process unrestricted access to system resources within its namespace.
rules.remediation: Set securityContext.appArmorProfile.type to RuntimeDefault or Localhost. For pre-1.30 manifests, set annotation container.apparmor.security.beta.kubernetes.io/<name> to runtime/default.

rules.description: procMount is set to Unmasked
rules.risk: Unmasked procMount exposes the full /proc filesystem inside the container without default masking of sensitive paths (/proc/kcore, /proc/sys, /proc/sysrq-trigger). This leaks host kernel information and opens kernel tuning interfaces.
rules.remediation: Set securityContext.procMount to Default or remove it entirely.

rules.description: subPath or subPathExpr used with a hostPath volume
rules.risk: Using subPath with hostPath volumes is vulnerable to symlink race conditions (CVE-2017-1002101, CVE-2021-25741). An attacker who can create symlinks in the volume may escape the intended subdirectory and access the entire host filesystem.
rules.remediation: Avoid using subPath with hostPath volumes. Use separate volumes or mount the entire path read-only instead.

rules.description: Pod enables shareProcessNamespace
rules.risk: When shareProcessNamespace is true, all containers in the pod share a single PID namespace. Processes in one container can see and signal processes in sibling containers. Combined with CAP_SYS_PTRACE, this enables cross-container process injection.
rules.remediation: Set spec.shareProcessNamespace to false unless cross-container process visibility is strictly required.
